material: # How to Make Money Trading with Candlestick Charts — Balkrishna Sadekar (Vision
note: Piercing pattern and morning star require identifying the exact prior red candle's midpoint to confirm reclaim — multi-bar pattern logic not expressible in the single-bar condition grammar.

material: # How to Make Money Trading with Candlestick Charts — Balkrishna Sadekar (Vision
note: Support/resistance zones and key price levels (e.g., prior swing lows, round numbers) require chart-structure recognition not available as an indicator series.

material: # How to Make Money Trading with Candlestick Charts — Balkrishna Sadekar (Vision
note: Pattern context quality (how extended the prior decline is, whether it is a first or third test of support) requires discretionary trend-structure reading.

material: # How to Make Money Trading with Candlestick Charts — Balkrishna Sadekar (Vision
note: Confirmation that a pattern forms 'at' a moving average rather than merely above/below it is a proximity judgment; a simple greater/less-than comparison captures direction but not proximity intent precisely.

material: # How to Make Money Trading with Candlestick Charts — Balkrishna Sadekar (Vision
note: Sadekar's emphasis that patterns against the larger trend are low-probability implies a multi-timeframe trend filter (e.g., weekly trend), which is not available in daily-bar-only grammar.

material: # A Beginner's Guide to Day Trading Online — Toni Turner (Adams Media, 2nd ed. 2
note: Principle 1 (trade with the weekly trend): requires a weekly-timeframe indicator or weekly SMA; daily-bar grammar only has daily SMAs. A crude proxy (close > sma_200) is already used by other rules but does not faithfully represent 'weekly trend direction'.

material: # A Beginner's Guide to Day Trading Online — Toni Turner (Adams Media, 2nd ed. 2
note: Principle 4 (minimum 2:1 reward-to-risk before entry): the R:R ratio depends on the distance from entry to identified support, which is a per-trade discretionary judgment not expressible as a fixed indicator condition; the grammar encodes stop_pct and target_pct statically, so the filter 'skip if R:R < 2' cannot be enforced dynamically.

material: # A Beginner's Guide to Day Trading Online — Toni Turner (Adams Media, 2nd ed. 2
note: Principle 6 (fixed-percentage position sizing, scale into competence with small size): portfolio-level risk management (% of equity per trade, position sizing) is outside the per-rule indicator grammar.

material: # Trading in the Zone — Mark Douglas (Prentice Hall, 2000)
note: Think in probabilities: a single trade's outcome is meaningless; only the distribution over many trades matters. Never judge a method on one trade. — No indicator grammar exists for 'evaluate rules only over distributions, not individual outcomes.'

material: # Trading in the Zone — Mark Douglas (Prentice Hall, 2000)
note: Predefine the risk of every trade before entry and accept it completely so no outcome causes hesitation or revenge trading. — This is an execution-discipline constraint, not a signal condition; stop_pct in any rule partially encodes the predefine-risk idea but the psychological acceptance component is untestable.

material: # Trading in the Zone — Mark Douglas (Prentice Hall, 2000)
note: Consistency requires executing the edge every time it appears — skipping valid signals out of fear, or taking invalid ones out of greed, destroys the measured edge. — Signal-skipping and impulsive additions are behavioral deviations invisible to a rule-based backtest.

material: # Trading in the Zone — Mark Douglas (Prentice Hall, 2000)
note: Do not carry the last trade into the next: errors are pattern-level (repeated deviations from plan), not outcome-level (a losing trade taken correctly is a good trade). — Loss-sequence-driven behavior changes cannot be encoded in indicator conditions.

material: # Trading in the Zone — Mark Douglas (Prentice Hall, 2000)
note: The market owes you nothing; expectation of a specific outcome is the root of emotional interference. — Purely psychological; no testable market-data proxy exists.

material: # Trading in the Zone — Mark Douglas (Prentice Hall, 2000)
note: All five fundamental truths (anything can happen; you don't need to predict; wins/losses are randomly distributed within an edge; an edge is a probability indication; every moment is unique) are epistemological priors for how to interpret backtest results, not entry conditions. Entire book is untestable in this grammar.

material: # Stock Market Wizards — Jack D. Schwager (HarperBusiness, 2001)
note: Risk control as universal trait (principle 1): stop discipline and pre-entry stop-setting are execution/psychological rules, not entry conditions; the framework already encodes stop_pct but the *discipline* to honor it cannot be tested in a rule grammar.

material: # Stock Market Wizards — Jack D. Schwager (HarperBusiness, 2001)
note: Trade a method fitting your personality (principle 2): entirely psychological/behavioral — untestable in any indicator grammar.

material: # Stock Market Wizards — Jack D. Schwager (HarperBusiness, 2001)
note: Position sizing varies with conviction, drawdown regime, and losing streaks (principle 4): requires portfolio-level equity-curve state and subjective conviction assessment; no indicator proxy available in daily bar grammar.

material: # Stock Market Wizards — Jack D. Schwager (HarperBusiness, 2001)
note: Patience / selectivity (principle 5): the instruction to 'do nothing otherwise' is a filter on trade frequency, not an entry condition — cannot be expressed as a condition row.

material: # Stock Market Wizards — Jack D. Schwager (HarperBusiness, 2001)
note: Systematic record-keeping and review of losing trades (principle 6): a process discipline, not a market signal.

material: # Stock Market Wizards — Jack D. Schwager (HarperBusiness, 2001)
note: 'Sound base' quality (principle 7): requires visual/structural chart-pattern assessment (e.g., VCP, cup-with-handle consolidation depth and duration) beyond what highest_N / lowest_N alone can reliably encode; partially approximated but the full concept is untestable in this grammar.

material: # Stock Market Wizards — Jack D. Schwager (HarperBusiness, 2001)
note: Market-regime filter ('strong markets'): requires a broad-market internals indicator (advance/decline, % of stocks above 200-day MA, index trend) which is not available in the single-stock daily bar grammar.
